title: Assisted dying, revolution or ethical dilemma? French healthcare workers divided on new law
subhead: For some, it's a step in the right direction, but for others, it's an ethical minefield. Le Monde spoke with healthcare professionals, who will be granted a 'conscience clause' under the law passed by Parliament on Wednesday.

title: 'It's Mad Max on the ocean': The cocaine-loaded speedboats racing to Europe's shores

Morocco's frenzy for purchasing surveillance tools revealed by whistleblower
subhead: Keyloggers installed via USB stick, phones sold with pre-installed spyware, wiretapping and hidden cameras... In addition to the Pegasus spyware, Morocco has assembled a powerful arsenal of repressive tools that it deploys against journalists and political targets.

title: Pegasus Project: Five years after revelations about a global phone surveillance system, judicial investigation continues
subhead: Two former executives of Israeli company NSO Group, which developed the spyware, were questioned in France. While the Elysée Palace promised in 2021 to 'fully shed light' on the surveillance of six government ministers, Paris has since forged closer diplomatic ties with Morocco, despite accusations that Rabat orchestrated the spying.

title: Tour de France: Tim Merlier claims third stage win
subhead: The Belgian rider claimed victory again in a crash-marred sprint in stage 12.

title: Who are the people setting France's forests ablaze?
subhead: Nearly nine out of 10 forest fires in France are started by humans. Some do so deliberately, with the perpetrators often difficult to identify.

title: For Macron, the legalization of aid in dying meant a long personal journey
subhead: After doubting the need for new legislation, fearing he might upset religious leaders and concerned about dividing French society, the president eventually resolved to support the legislation, under pressure from MPs and public opinion.

title: 'We were meant to take as many jewels as we could': The confessions of two Louvre burglars
subhead: After months of silence, two men suspected of stealing the royal jewels described their involvement in detail during new interrogations in June. According to their statements, they were hired to carry out the heist for '€15,000 to €25,000.'
